title: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
subtitle: This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.
caption:
Cumulative number of objects launched into space

This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.

The line chart tracks the total accumulation of space objects from 1957 through 2025, with the vertical axis measuring the count of items from zero to 25,000. The data attributes launches to the country on whose behalf the object was sent, rather than the launching nation. The World aggregate line begins at zero in 1957 and rises steadily to approximately 5,000 by the year 2000, before curving upward more steeply after 2010 to reach a peak of 25,000 by the end of the timeline. The United States line follows a similar trajectory but at a lower magnitude, reaching roughly 16,000 by 2025. Russia maintains a position as the third-highest entity, with a line that rises to just under 4,000 and remains relatively flat compared to the global total. A cluster of nations including China, the United Kingdom, Japan, France, India, and Germany occupies the bottom section of the chart, with all lines staying below the 2,000 mark for the majority of the period. China shows a visible uptick near the end, crossing the 1,000 threshold, while the European Space Agency line remains near the baseline. The visual pattern displays a long period of gradual accumulation followed by a rapid acceleration in launch frequency during the most recent decade.
